title: From headlines to hard data: mapping the uneven impact of geopolitical risk in Europe
summary: ECB staff economists Martin Bijsterbosch, Matteo Falagiarda and Lucia Žídeková present a new news-based geopolitical risk indicator constructed from domestic EU newspaper sources, adapting the Caldara-Iacoviello (2022) methodology to a European context. The indicator shows that Russia's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine represented the largest perceived geopolitical shock in at least 20 years, with impacts varying by proximity to the conflict and pre-war energy dependence on Russia.
key_points: The indicator measures monthly share of articles referencing adverse geopolitical developments relative to total articles published, based on keywords covering war, conflict, terrorism, military buildup, and nuclear threats combined with tension/threat/risk/crisis terminology

Russia's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine was the largest perceived geopolitical shock for at least 20 years, compounding already high inflation, denting sentiment, increasing uncertainty, and triggering financial market volatility
Countries closer to the conflict and those with stronger pre-war economic and trade ties to Russia experienced higher geopolitical risk scores

Central and eastern European (CEE) countries were particularly affected due to energy-intensive production, high food/energy share in household spending, economic openness, and supply chain integration
Investment was hit particularly hard, declining approximately 3 percentage points in CEE countries between 2022-2024
Geopolitical risks contributed to market fragmentation and further strained already stressed supply chains across Europe
why_it_matters: This ECB research provides a new empirical tool for measuring how geopolitical shocks transmit differently across EU economies. For policymakers, the indicator helps identify which regions face elevated risk and may require targeted support. For markets, the differential exposure of CEE countries versus western European nations suggests asymmetric economic vulnerabilities that could affect ECB policy transmission and relative asset performance.
macro_context: The research demonstrates that geopolitical risk shocks had substantial and heterogeneous impacts on real GDP, investment, exports, and inflation across European economies from 2022-2024. CEE countries experienced the most severe investment drag (approximately 3 percentage point decline) due to elevated uncertainty driving up risk premia and discouraging long-term investment plans. The indicator provides evidence that proximity to the Ukraine conflict and pre-war Russian energy dependence were key transmission channels.
important_numbers: 3 percentage points (investment decline in CEE countries)
20 years (backdrop for largest geopolitical shock assessment)
notes: Source is a substantive ECB research blog post presenting original empirical work. The article was published approximately 4 months prior to TODAY (2026-03-31), so it remains relevant. Content was truncated at approximately 80% but key findings and methodology are present. No specific forward guidance or market trading signals provided; this is analytical research. Authors are ECB staff economists; findings represent research output rather than official ECB policy positions.
